meaning: Assuming that one's own subjective experience or interpretation of an event is objective reality for everyone. Analogy: Believing that because you find public speaking easy, anyone who is nervous about it is just not trying hard enough.
example: Learning calculus is incredibly easy and intuitive, so anyone who struggles with it must be lazy.

meaning: Exaggerating the virtues and happiness of a past era while ignoring its hardships and flaws. Analogy: Remembering your childhood summers as perfect sunny days, forgetting the sunburns, bee stings, and boredom.
example: Life in the middle ages was peaceful, simple, and full of community spirit, unlike our stressful modern world.

meaning: The tendency to believe, after an event has occurred, that you predicted or expected it all along. Analogy: Claiming you knew exactly which horse would win a race, but only saying so after the race has finished.
example: Once the stock market crashed, he told everyone that he had known for months that the crash was coming.

meaning: Believing that vague, general personality descriptions apply specifically and accurately to oneself. Analogy: Reading a generic horoscope in a newspaper and feeling shocked by how perfectly it describes your day.
example: The personality test said I value honesty but sometimes feel insecure, which is incredibly accurate!

meaning: The tendency to retroactively ascribe positive attributes to an option one has selected, while downplaying the flaws. Analogy: Defending a car you bought despite its constant engine trouble, insisting it was still the best choice.
example: After buying the expensive laptop, she constantly praised its keyboard while ignoring its terrible battery life.

meaning: The tendency to overestimate the significance of small clusters of patterns in random data. Analogy: Seeing a cloud shaped like a teapot and believing it is a sign from the universe.
example: I had three dreams about water this week, so there must be a flood coming to our town.

meaning: The predisposition to view the past favorably and the future negatively, believing that society is in inevitable decline. Analogy: An elderly person complaining that young people have no manners and the world is going to ruin, forgetting that every generation has said this for thousands of years.
example: In the old days, everyone worked hard and cared for each other; today, society has completely collapsed morally.

meaning: The inability to appreciate how much your decision-making is influenced by your current emotional or physical state, or to understand others' visceral states. Analogy: Shopping for groceries while full and assuming you will never want to eat snacks again.
example: Now that he is calm, he cannot understand why he became so angry during the argument yesterday.

meaning: The tendency to forget information that can be easily found online using search engines. Analogy: Forgetting your best friend's phone number because it is saved in your contacts list.
example: I no longer remember historical dates because I know I can look them up on my phone in five seconds.

meaning: The phenomenon where a person's recall of episodic memories becomes less accurate because of post-event information. Analogy: Watching a video of a car crash, being asked how fast the cars were going when they 'smashed' into each other, and later remembering broken glass that wasn't actually there.
example: After talking to other witnesses who mentioned a red car, the witness mistakenly recalled seeing a red car at the crime scene.

meaning: The tendency to register and dwell on negative stimuli more than positive stimuli. Analogy: Receiving ten compliments and one criticism, and spending the entire evening worrying about the criticism.
example: Despite all the positive reviews of the restaurant, I couldn't stop thinking about the one bad review I read.

meaning: Evaluating an experience based on how it felt at its peak (highest intensity) and its end, rather than the average of the entire experience. Analogy: Rating a two-week vacation as terrible because you lost your luggage on the flight home, despite having a wonderful time for thirteen days.
example: The painful dental procedure was rated as less unpleasant because the dentist ended with a few minutes of painless checkups.

meaning: The tendency to remember interrupted or uncompleted tasks better than completed ones. Analogy: Remembering the questions you got wrong or didn't finish on an exam much more clearly than the ones you answered correctly.
example: I cannot sleep because I keep thinking about the half-finished report sitting on my office desk.

meaning: Difficulty in imagining what it is like for someone else not to know something that you already know. Analogy: A software engineer explaining how to use a computer to a grandmother using complex coding jargon, forgetting she doesn't know what a 'compiler' is.
example: The professor explained the math concept so quickly that the beginners were completely lost; he had forgotten how difficult it was to learn it for the first time.

meaning: The enhancement or diminishment of perception as a result of exposure to a recently observed stimulus of lesser or greater value. Analogy: Placing one hand in hot water and one in cold water, then placing both in lukewarm water; the hand from the cold water feels hot, while the other feels cold.
example: The average-looking house looked like a luxury mansion because the real estate agent showed us three run-down houses right before it.

meaning: A cognitive bias where something you recently noticed or learned seems to appear everywhere with improbable frequency. Analogy: Learning the word 'ubiquitous' and suddenly seeing it in three different books and two articles in the same week.
example: Right after I bought a blue car, I started seeing blue cars on every street corner, believing everyone was buying them.
